coredata.dc:description: The use of thermal conversion to convert Chinese patent medicines (CPM) residues into porous carbon-based materials and carbon dots (CDs) has effectively resolved the challenges associated with resource waste and environmental pollution while enhancing the added value of CPM residues. In this study, biochar and N-doped CDs with high quantum yield (QY) were simultaneously prepared from fermentation residues by employing a hydrothermal carbonization method without any chemical reagents. The obtained biochar had remarkable adsorption capacity, with an iodine adsorption value of 693.22 mg g–1. The QY of N-doped CDs could reach to 36.17 % by the mean of self-doping due to the high nitrogen content of the fermentation residues. Considering the structural integrity and permeable porous structure of the two-dimensional leaf-like zeolitic imidazole framework (ZIF-L), N-doped CDs were encapsulated into ZIF-L to form novel fluorescent composites (N-doped CDs@ZIF-L). Moreover, N-doped CDs@ZIF-L as a novel fluorescent probe could be efficiently quenched in the presence of tetracycline (TC), doxycycline (DOC), and chlortetracycline (CTC) by static quenching and the inner filter effect. Notably, the fluorescent probe exhibited a good linear relationship with the TC, DOC, and CTC at 0–50, 0–50, and 0–45 μM, respectively, with limits of detection of 0.025, 0.033, and 0.027 μM, respectively. This probe could be employed to detect TC antibiotics in honey and milk, suggesting that it had the great potential for applications in environmental monitoring and food safety.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The use of thermal conversion to convert Chinese patent medicines (CPM) residues into porous carbon-based materials and carbon dots (CDs) has effectively resolved the challenges associated with resource waste and environmental pollution while enhancing the added value of CPM residues. In this study, biochar and N-doped CDs with high quantum yield (QY) were simultaneously prepared from fermentation residues by employing a hydrothermal carbonization method without any chemical reagents. The obtained biochar had remarkable adsorption capacity, with an iodine adsorption value of 693.22 mg g–1. The QY of N-doped CDs could reach to 36.17 % by the mean of self-doping due to the high nitrogen content of the fermentation residues. Considering the structural integrity and permeable porous structure of the two-dimensional leaf-like zeolitic imidazole framework (ZIF-L), N-doped CDs were encapsulated into ZIF-L to form novel fluorescent composites (N-doped CDs@ZIF-L). Moreover, N-doped CDs@ZIF-L as a novel fluorescent probe could be efficiently quenched in the presence of tetracycline (TC), doxycycline (DOC), and chlortetracycline (CTC) by static quenching and the inner filter effect. Notably, the fluorescent probe exhibited a good linear relationship with the TC, DOC, and CTC at 0–50, 0–50, and 0–45 μM, respectively, with limits of detection of 0.025, 0.033, and 0.027 μM, respectively. This probe could be employed to detect TC antibiotics in honey and milk, suggesting that it had the great potential for applications in environmental monitoring and food safety.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, traditional Chinese medicine (TCM) has attracted much attention worldwide owing to its advantages such as low toxicity, mild medicinal properties, and effective treatment of difficult diseases. However, the annual production of TCM residues reached 30 million tons in China with pharmaceutical companies producing one-third of the residues. And Chinese patent medicines (CPM) accounts for about 70 % of the total TCM residues [1,2]. Common treatments for CPM residues such as landfilling, stacking, and burning may negatively impact the utilization of resources, environment, and human health. Existing biotransformation technologies such as composting, animal feed production, edible mushroom cultivation, and biogas production [3–5] exhibit the disadvantages such as low energy efficiency, high cost, and challenging storage. Simultaneously, large amounts of organic substances in CPM residues such as cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin are wasted. Therefore, the repurposing of CPM residues has become a critical problem.

CPM residues contain a significant amount of organic matter that could be used as raw materials to prepare biochar and carbon dots (CDs). The thermochemical methods for converting CPM residues into carbon materials include pyrolysis [6–8] and hydrothermal carbonization [9,10]. The latter has attracted much attention owing to its low carbonization temperature and the absence of energy-intensive pre-drying steps. During hydrothermal carbonization, the waste undergoes a series of organic reactions, such as hydrolysis, dehydration, decarboxylation, aromatization, and recondensation, producing solid- and liquid-phase products [11–13]. Typically, the obtained solid product (biochar) is used in the adsorption, catalysis, and hydrogen storage fields owing to its high surface area, high porosity, and abundant active sites. However, the liquid suspension is typically wasted, which causes not only water pollution and waste, but also a significant amount of nanoscale carbon materials; that is, CDs in the liquid phase, which cannot be utilized. In addition, there have been few reports on the simultaneous production of CDs and biochar from biomass materials [14–16]. Thus, implementing a green synthetic process to obtain high-value solid- and liquid-phase carbon materials is a practical approach. CDs are zero-dimensional (0D) carbon materials with a particle size less than 10 nm and comprise sp2/sp3 carbon skeletons and abundant functional groups [17–19]. The high number of surface functional groups, such as carboxyl, amino, and hydroxyl groups, give them excellent water solubility and make them easy to form composites with other materials. In addition, the abundance of functional groups and high specific surface areas make CDs easily accessible to other substances, making them promising candidates for sensing various substances. However, CDs tend to aggregate during electron transfer, which leads to fluorescence quenching [20–22]. Consequently, encapsulating CDs in different types of matrix materials to modulate the fluorescence properties of the composite materials and achieve coupling effects between the host and guest is an effective approach to address these issues.

Porous zeolitic imidazole framework-8 (ZIF-8) is a metal-organic framework material prepared by the coordination of Zn2+ and 2-methylimidazole. It has been universally applied in sensing [23,24], gas separation [25], catalysis [26], and other fields because of its advantages, such as a large specific surface area, uniform pore size, and adjustable morphology [27]. In particular, the closed pore channels significantly reduced the aggregation and migration of CDs, leading to the good dispersion and protection of the CDs. In addition, compared with traditional three-dimensional (3D) materials, two-dimensional (2D) ZIF materials (ZIF-L) have a larger outer surface area and thinner features, thus improving the electron transfer ability and exposing more active sites [28,29]. Therefore, the development of CDs and 2D ZIF-L materials shows the enormous potential for the identification and removal of pollutants.

Tetracycline antibiotics (TCs) are broad-spectrum antibiotics typically employed to treat Gram-positive and -negative bacteria. Moreover, it has been applied in animal husbandry owing to its low cost and beneficial effects. However, the abuse of TCs in animal husbandry leads to bacterial resistance and damage to teeth, liver, and kidneys [30,31]. Currently, universal methods are available for detecting TCs, including capillary electrophoresis [32], high-performance liquid chromatography [33], spectrophotometry [34], and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay [35]. These methods are cumbersome, time-consuming, and expensive; therefore, there is a need for a rapid, simple, sensitive, and accurate method for monitoring TCs.

In this study, high-quantum-yield N-doped CDs and biochar were prepared simultaneously from fermented residues using a green and simple hydrothermal carbonization method to achieve the comprehensive utilization of waste biomass, enhance economic value, and reduce environmental pollution. First, the adsorption performance of biochar was investigated based on its iodine value, which provided ideas for the subsequent adsorption experiments. In addition, the synthesized biomass CDs were encapsulated into 2D sheet-like cross-linked ZIF-L to enhance stability and expose more active sites during monitoring. The synthesized N-doped CDs@ZIF-L were effectively quenched in the presence of TCs. To verify the authenticity of the detection results, the real tests of N-doped CDs@ZIF-L for sensing TCs were conducted in common foods such as honey and milk. Hence, this study will promote the comprehensive utilization of waste biomass and reduce environmental pollution, providing a theoretical basis for obtaining more valuable products. Moreover, the fluorescence sensor constructed based on the composite material could be used to detect TCs, demonstrating its potential for environmental monitoring and food pollutant control.

The fermentation residues are the fermentation wet bacteria produced by plate and frame filtration of fermentation broth in the process of producing CPM, which obtained from Shandong Runde Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shandong, Taian). Zn(NO3)2·6H2O (AR, 99 %), 2-methylimidazole (98 %, Hmim), tetracycline hydrochloride (96 %, TC), doxycycline hydrochloride (≥95 %, DOC), chlortetracycline hydrochloride (95 %, CTC) were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. AlCl3 (99.0 %), ZnCl2 (99.0 %), CrCl3·6H2O (AR), MnCl2·4H2O (AR, 99.0 %), NiCl2·6H2O (AR, 99.0 %), PbSO4 (AR, 98.0 %), potassium iodide (99.8 %), potassium iodate (99.5 %), soluble starch (AR), sodium thiosulfate (99 %), potassium iodate (99.8 %, KIO3), and potassium iodide (99.5 %, KI) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd. Ethanol (AR, 95 %), KNO3 (AR, 99.0 %), NaCl (AR, 99.5 %), Ca(NO3)2·4H2O (AR, 99.0 %), FeCl3·6H2O (AR, 99.0 %), FeCl2·4H2O (AR, 98.0 %), CoCl2·6H2O (AR, 99.0 %), L-histidine (BR, 98.5 %, L-His), glycine (BR, 99.0 %, Gly), L-lysine (BR, 98.5 %, L-Lys), sucrose (BR, 99.0 %, Suc), L-glutamic acid (BR, 98.5 %, L-Glu), DL-tartaric acid (BR, 99.0 %, DL-Tar), and D(-)-fructose (BR, 98.0 %, D(-)-Fru), sodium thiosulfate pentahydrate (AR, ≥99.0 %, Na2S2O3), and soluble starch (AR) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Beijing, China).

The physicochemical properties of the fermented bacterial residue were obtained using an organic elemental analyzer (EA, Vario MACRO cube, Germany) and a coal industry analyzer (SDTGA5000, China). The morphologies and structures of the biochar, 2D leaf-like ZIF-L, and N-doped CDs@ZIF-L were characterized using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Zeiss Supra 55, Germany). The fluorescence properties of N-doped CDs were analyzed using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi F-4700, Japan), and the absorption spectra were measured using a UV-Vis spectrophotometer (MAPADA P5, China). The morphology features of N-doped CDs were observed using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI Tecnai F20, USA). The functional groups of N-doped CDs were detected using a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR, Thermo Nicolet 6700, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were collected by an Al-Kα radiation source on a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha system (USA). The crystal structure of N-doped CDs was further characterized using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Malvern Panalytical, Netherlands). The content of amorphous carbon and graphitic carbon in N-doped CDs was analyzed by Raman spectroscopy (Raman, Horiba LabRAM HR Evolution, Japan).

The pore structures of ZIF-L and N-doped CDs@ZIF-L were measured using a fully automated surface area and pore size analyzer (Micromeritics ASAP 2460, USA), and the Brunner-Emmet-Teller (BET) theory was used to calculate the specific surface area. The total volume (Vt) was obtained at P/P0 = 0.99, and the pore size distribution was calculated using the dental functional theory. All samples were degassed at 150 °C for 12 h before analysis. The stability of N-doped CDs, ZIF-L, and N-doped CDs@ZIF-L samples was analyzed by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA, TA TGA 550, USA) in the temperature range of 25–1000 °C, with a heating rate of 10 °C min–1 and a protective gas flow rate of 50 mL min–1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of biochar and N-doped CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, dry the damp fermented residues in a 65 °C air-drying oven for convenient storage, and then grind the fermented residues until it has no particle sensation. Secondly, 6.0 g fermented residues powder was mixed into 60 mL of ultrapure water for 15 min, then transfer the above mixture to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. Next, place the high-pressure reactor in an oven and heat it to the reaction temperature (140 °C, 160 °C, 180 °C, and 200 °C) for 6 h, and maintaining it at the optimal temperature for different dwell times (3 h, 6 h, 9 h, and 12 h). After the reaction has already finished, the mixture was cooled to room temperature and the reaction products were collected. The collected mixture was centrifuged at 8500 rpm for 10 min to separate the supernatant from the residual solid. The obtained supernatant was filtered by 0.22 µm filter membrane to remove large particles. Subsequently, the sample was dialyzed for 48 h in the dialysis membrane (MWCO=1000 Da). After dialysis, the collected solution was freeze-dried to obtain brown solid CDs. And the CDs with highest quantum yield (QY) were called N-doped CDs. Next, the obtained solid-phase products under different reaction conditions were washed three times with water and ethanol, followed by overnight drying at 60 °C in an oven to obtain the final biochar products.

The iodine absorption value showed the development of pore structure of the biochar below 1.0 nm according to the test methods of wooden activated carbon determination of iodine absorption number (GB/T 12496.8-2015). The iodine absorption value also represented the adsorption ability for small molecule impurities below 1.0 nm diameter. The titration method was used to measure the remaining concentration of Iodine using 0.1 mol L–1 Na2S2O3 and starch as an endpoint indicator. Extractly 10 mL of 5 % HCl was added to 0.2–0.5 g biochar and boiled for 5 min. After cooling, 50 mL standard iodine solution (0.1 mol L–1) was added to the suspension and vigorously shaken for 15 min, and the biochar was separated from the iodine solution using a syringe filter (0.45 µm). The filtrate iodine solution was titrated with Na2S2O3 and starch to quantify the residual and adsorbed iodine. The iodine absorption number was calculated by the following equation,
(1)

Where A is the iodine value (mg g–1), C 1 is the concentration of iodine solution (mol L–1), C 2 is the concentration of Na2S2O3 (mol L–1), V 1 is the titration reading (mL), M is biochar mass (g), D is the correction factor.

The photoluminescence quantum yield (QY) was measured using the quinine sulfate (in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution, QY=0.54) as the reference and was calculated with the following equation [36,37],
(2)

where Φ, I, η and A represent the QY, integrated fluorescence intensity, the refractive index of the solvent and the absorbance at the optimum excitation wavelength, respectively. The subscript “R” refers to the reference sample. For the CDs solution and reference solution, η/η R= 1.

The 2D leaf-shaped ZIF-L were prepared according to the reported method in the literature[38]. Firstly, 1.6625 g Hmim was dissolved in 50 mL of ultrapure water to form solution A, and 0.7732 g Zn(NO3)2·6H2O was dissolved in 50 mL of ultrapure water to form solution B. The above solutions both were subjected to sonication for 10 min, following that solution A was added to solution B and sonicated for 4 min. The resulting solution was then allowed to age for 16 h at room temperature. The obtained sample was washed three times with ethanol via centrifugation (8,500 rpm, 5 min) and subsequently dried overnight in a vacuum drying oven at 60 ℃, resulting in white 2D ZIF-L powder.

The N-doped CDs@ZIF-L were synthesized by following the method reported in the literature, with minor modifications[39,40]. Initially, 10 mg N-doped CDs was added to 50 mL of ultrapure water, and 1.6625 g Hmim was dissolved in the water and magnetically stirred for 20 min at room temperature to form a uniform solution A. 0.7732 g Zn(NO3)2·6 H2O was dissolved in 50 mL of ultrapure water to form solution B. Solution A was quickly added to solution B, sonicated for 5 min, and then the mixed solution was aged at 35 ℃ for 48 h. Finally, the sample was washed three times with ethanol by centrifugation (8,500 rpm, 5 min) and dried overnight in a vacuum drying oven at 60 ℃, resulting in faint yellow N-doped CDs@ZIF-L powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TCs by N-doped CDs@ZIF-L
ce:section.ce:para: First of all, N-doped CDs@ZIF-L were diluted into phosphate-buffered saline solution (PBS, pH=7.0). Next, 1.5 mL of the above solution (1.5 mg mL–1) was mixed with 0.5 mL of different standard concentration TCs solutions (TC, DOC, CTC). After a thorough mixing, the fluorescence intensity of the mixture was measured at an excitation wavelength of 310 nm and room temperature for 5 min. To investigate the selectivity of the N-doped CDs@ZIF-L solution, the 50 μM other substances (Cr6+, Cr3+, Al3+, K+, Na+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Fe2+, AA, Gly, DL-Tar, L-His, L-Glu, D(-)-Fru, Cit, Suc, Glu) were added into the N-doped CDs@ZIF-L solution, and further the mixtures were measured using the same method as described above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pretreatment and analysis of TCs in food samples
ce:section.ce:para: Milk and honey were selected as the actual samples, both purchased from a supermarket at Shandong University[41,42]. The processing methods for milk and honey samples were similar to those previously described. 10 mL milk was added to a mixture of 10 mL acetonitrile and water (v/v = 4:1). The solution was then shaken by hand for 3 min and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove the precipitate. The obtained supernatant was then diluted 20 times with PBS solution (pH = 7.0). For the honey samples (1 g), 5 mL PBS solution was used for dilution. The diluted honey sample was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 5 min and filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane to remove solid suspensions. Three types of TCs at concentrations of 20 and 40 μM were added to the milk and honey samples. This analysis method was consistent with the above procedure.

The iodine adsorption value was employed to characterize the adsorption performance of the adsorbents, primarily reflecting the biochar adsorption capacity for small molecules. During preparation, hydrothermal temperature is an important parameter for determining the physicochemical properties of biochar. Fig. 1(a) illustrateed that as the reaction temperature increased from 140 to 200°C, the iodine value of biochar gradually increased from 458.16 to 693.22 mg g–1. As shown in Fig. 1(c)-(d) and (f)-(g), the micropores of biochar become more uniform, the number of it increased and the degree of biochar carbonization was enhanced with the increase of reaction temperature, which resulted in an increase of the iodine value[43]. Fig. 1(b) demonstrated the impact of dwell time on the iodine value of biochar. As the dwell time increased from 3 to 12 h, the iodine value initially increased rapidly and then decreased gradually. When the dwell time was 6 h, the iodine value reached its maximum at 693.22 mg g–1. This phenomenon occurred because a prolonged dwell time may damage the pore structure of the biochar and cause small pores to collapse into larger pores. In Fig. 1(d)-(e) and (g)-(h), it was observed that with the increase of dwell time and carbonization degree, the morphology of biochar transformed from micron-particles to flakes-likely pores, which further demonstrated the reason for the decrease of iodine value.

The elemental and industrial analyses results of the fermented residue are summarized in Table 1. It can be observed in Table 1 that the original residues primarily comprised C, H, O, and N, with carbon and nitrogen accounting for 47.89 % and 12.31 %, respectively. The high carbon content in the fermented residues was beneficial for the formation of carbon skeletons during the reaction process, and the high nitrogen content was advantageous for self-passivation of the N-doped CDs to improve their QY. During the reaction process, the nitrogen element was introduced into the CDs through decomposition, self-polymerization, aggregation, carbonization and self-passivation. Therefore, the fermented residues, relying on the high carbon and nitrogen content, were used to prepare high-quantum-yield CDs as carbon source and nitrogen source. In this study, the preparation conditions for green hydrothermal carbonization were determined using the QY as an index (Fig. S1). The different reaction temperature and dwell time were very important for the formation and fluorescent properties of CDs. Reaction temperature significantly affected the preparation of CDs. Low reaction temperature was not enough to change the surface of CDs. However, high temperature might lead to excessive carbonization and decomposition of CDs, resulting in the decrease of the QY. In this study, the QY of N-doped CDs was investigated at reaction temperatures of 140, 160, 180, and 200 °C when the dwell time was fixed at 6 h ( Fig. 2(a)). With an increase in the reaction temperature, the QY gradually increased from 7.88 % to 20.12 %, which may be due to the increase of the fluorescent polymer chains. If the dwell time was prolonged, the QY of CDs would reduce because prolonged continuous heating may lead to excessive carbonization and the formation of by-products. We then explored the effect of dwell time on the QY. With an increase in the dwell time (Fig. 2(b)), the QY reached a maximum at 36.17 % when the dwell time was 12 h under the optimal reaction temperature. These results were consistent with those of previous studies, indicating that the reaction was inadequate at short heating times and low temperatures. Consequently, the optimal preparation conditions for N-doped CDs were a hydrothermal temperature of 200 °C and a dwell time of 12 h. Consequently, the prepared CDs at 200 ℃ for 12 h were called N-doped CDs, which were further characterized to ensure the morphologies, structures and optical properties and used to prepare the N-doped CDs@ZIF-L.

TEM images ( Fig. 3(a)) revealed that the synthesized N-doped CDs were uniformly dispersed quasi-spherical nanoparticles with particle sizes ranging between 0.83 and 2.61 nm (150 randomly selected CDs were statistically analyzed), and an average diameter of 1.35 ± 0.35 nm (inset (1) in Fig. 3(a)). High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (inset (2) in Fig. 3(a)) demonstrated a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, similar to the (100) crystal plane of graphite, which was consistent with previous studies on CDs[44]. In addition, the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of the N-doped CDs (inset (3) in Fig. 3(a)) indicated the amorphous nature of carbon. The crystal structure of the N-doped CDs was investigated using XRD and Raman spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 3(b), a broad peak at 2θ = 20.27° appeared, which was the characteristic diffraction peak of carbon. Furthermore, the degree of graphitization of the N-doped CDs was determined using Raman spectroscopy (Fig. 3(c)). The D peak at 1378 cm–1 was associated with the vibration of sp3 hybridized carbons, whereas the G peak at 1560 cm–1 corresponded to the vibration of sp2 hybridized carbons. The intensity ratio of the disordered D peak to the ordered G peak (ID/IG) was 1.04, suggesting that the higher the number of N-doped CDs defects, the higher the ID/IG ratio. The 2D peak of CDs was observed at 2945 cm–1, corresponding to the graphene layer.

The functional groups on the surface of the N-doped CDs were identified using FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 3(d)). The absorption band at 1393 cm–1 and 1453 cm–1 were mainly attributed to the C–N and CN stretching vibration, respectively, suggesting the successful doping of nitrogen into the CDs[45]. The absorption peaks between 3000 and 3500 cm–1 were mainly caused by the O–H and N–H stretching vibrations, whereas the absorption band at 2930 cm–1 was mainly ascribed to the stretching vibration of C–H. The CO and CC functional groups were located at 1663 cm–1. The broad band at approximately 1106 cm–1 was caused by the C–O bending vibration. The results indicated that the surfaces of the N-doped CDs contained hydrophilic functional groups, such as amino, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups, demonstrating their good water solubility.

XPS technique was used to further characterize the properties of chemical bonds and functional groups on the surface of N-doped CDs-200 ℃− 6 h and N-doped CDs-200 ℃− 12 h. In the full scan XPS spectra of N-doped CDs-200 ℃− 12 h ( Fig. 4(e)), C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s signals were around 285, 400 and 531 eV, respectively. Observed N peak confirmed that N was doped into these N-doped CDs-200 ℃− 12 h . In addition, the C peak intensity turned stronger while O peak intensity turned weaker with elevating dwell time (Fig. 4). The carbon to oxygen atomic ratio increased from 5.56 to 7.67, which indicated that carbonization degree of CDs increased with the elevation of the dwell time. Whereas the N peak intensity kept almost unchanged with increasing dwell time, suggesting that elevating dwell time did not increase nitrogen doping degree significantly. The high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 4(f)) exhibited three distinct peaks at 284.60 eV (C–C/CC), 258.90 eV (C–O/C–N), and 287.82 eV (CO) [46]. The peaks at 531.17 and 532.69 eV in the high-resolution O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 4(g)) were attributed to the CO and C–O bonds, respectively [47]. In the N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 4(h)), peaks were observed at 399.58 eV (C–N–C) and 401.14 eV (N–H) [48]. And the chemical bonds of N-doped CDs-200 ℃− 6 h were similar with that of N-doped CDs-200 ℃− 12 h in Fig. 4(a)-(d). The above results demonstrated that N atoms had doped into N-doped CDs framework, and there existed hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl and amide groups. The QY of N-doped CDs depended largely on the number of fluorescent polymer chains, and the competition between fluorescent polymer chains formation on the surface of N-doped CDs and carbon core growth leaded to the change in number of fluorescent polymer chains [49]. With the increase of dwell time, it was speculated that polymer-like N-doped CDs were gradually carbonized to form carbon core-containing N-doped CDs and QY increased owing to the increase of fluorescent polymer chains, as a result of faster extension and formation fluorescent polymer chains than its consumption. Therefore, the QY of N-doped CDs-200 ℃− 12 h was higher than that of N-doped CDs-200 ℃− 6 h.

In addition, the optical properties of the N-doped CDs were characterized. As shown in Fig. 5(a), the aqueous solution of the N-doped CDs exhibited pale yellow color under sunlight and blue color under UV light. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of N-doped CDs (Fig. 5(a)) exhibited a weak shoulder peak at 250–300 nm, which was attributed to a π–π * transition within the conjugated skeleton of the carbon core in the N-doped CDs [37,50]. The 2D fluorescence spectrum of the N-doped CDs (Fig. 5(b)) exhibited two fluorescent centers at (264, 420) nm and (330, 415) nm. However, the fluorescence peak at the first fluorescence center was not well-defined, and the second fluorescence center was selected as the characteristic peak. As shown in Fig. 5(c) and (d), the N-doped CDs exhibited an excitation wavelength-dependent emission, where the emission peak shifted from 415 nm to 510 nm and the fluorescence color changed from blue to green as the excitation wavelength increased from 330 nm to 460 nm. This excitation-dependent behavior could be explained by the differences in the particle size and emission trap states [44,51]. The introduction of oxygen and nitrogen functional groups as excitation energy traps resulted in different defects in the N-doped CDs, leading to different fluorescence properties.

The synthesized N-doped CDs also exhibited a good optical stability. As shown in Fig. 5(e), the fluorescence intensity of the N-doped CDs remained above 80 % of the original fluorescence intensity in NaCl solution–ranging from 0 to 3.0 M, indicating good salt resistance of the N-doped CDs under high salt conditions. Fig. 5(f) showed the fluorescence intensity of the N-doped CDs solution at different UV-irradiation times. When the prepared N-doped CDs were continuously irradiated lasting for 60 min under 365 nm light, the fluorescent intensity of N-doped CDs remained above 80 %, suggesting that the N-doped CDs have the good anti-blenching properties within 60 min. These results demonstrated that N-doped CDs solutions could maintain good stability even under harsh and complex environments, making them suitable for practical applications.

The morphologies and structures of the synthesized ZIF-L and N-doped CDs@ZIF-L were observed using SEM images. As shown in Fig. 6(a), ZIF-L had a leaf-like structure with a length, width, and thickness of approximately 8.46, 2.84, and 0.22 µm, respectively. Some of the leaf-like structures intersected each other, forming a cross or flower shape, indicating the successful preparation of 2D ZIF-L. Moreover, after it was doped by N-doped CDs, the morphology and size of the ZIF-L changed. The size of the N-doped CDs@ZIF-L was determined to be 4.182, 1.899, and 0.166 µm (Fig. 6(b)), with blunted ends on both sides of the leaf-like material. The mechanism underlying this change is not supported by the literature and requires further investigation.

The peak positions of ZIF-L and N-doped CDs@ZIF-L were compared to the standard card of ZIF-8 using XRD (Fig. 6(c)). The results indicated that the strongest peak of 2D ZIF-L appeared at 18.0° on the (222) plane rather than at 7.4° on the (020) plane. Moreover, compared with ZIF-8, the crystal structure change of ZIF-L resulted in stronger secondary peaks (11.0°, 15.0°, and 17.0°), indicating that the crystal structure of ZIF-L was more complex. Owing to its low carbon content, N-doped CDs@ZIF-L did not exhibit clear carbon peak structures. However, sharp diffraction peaks appeared at 7.4° (011), 10.4° (112), and 12.8° (222) in the XRD patterns of ZIF-L and N-doped CDs@ZIF-L, suggesting that the obtained materials were highly crystalline and free of impurities.

The pore characteristics of the prepared ZIF-L and N-doped CDs@ZIF-L were investigated by N2 adsorption–desorption experiments to determine the position of the N-doped CDs in the composite materials. The specific surface area of the N-doped CDs@ZIF-L (7.80 m2 g–1) was smaller than that of ZIF-L (9.96 m2 g–1), indicating that N-doped CDs were encapsulated in the cavities of ZIF-L, blocking some of the pores of ZIF-L, resulting in a reduced adsorption capacity and BET surface area (Fig. 6(d) and Table S1).

The thermal stability of the composite material was investigated using TGA curves. When the temperature was less than 100 °C, the mass loss of the CDs was minimal, mainly because of the loss of water. When the temperature increased from 100 to 200 °C, the weight of the N-doped CDs began to decrease, demonstrating the dissociation of its surface functional groups and small molecules. From 200 to 400 °C, the weight of the CDs significantly decreased, mainly owing to the dissociation of the polymer cages and carbon core of the N-doped CDs [52], demonstrating the good thermal stability of N-doped CDs at low temperatures. The first stage of mass loss of N-doped CDs@ZIF-L occurred between 200 and 300 °C, with a mass loss of approximately 22 %, mainly owing to the removal of residual solvents and the decomposition of amide groups in N-doped CDs. The mass loss of more than 500 °C was mainly attributed to the collapse of the framework, suggesting the good thermal stability of the N-doped CDs@ZIF-L, which was consistent with previous studies [53].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TCs by N-doped CDs@ZIF-L

ce:section.ce:para: First, the optimal concentration of the N-doped CDs@ZIF-L for detection was determined. N-doped CDs@ZIF-L solutions with concentrations ranging from 0.5 to 3.5 mg mL–1 were investigated. As shown in Fig. 7(a), when the concentration of N-doped CDs@ZIF-L was 3.0 mg mL−1 and 3.5 mg mL−1, the fluorescent intensity of N-doped CDs @ ZIF-L decreased slightly, but the decrease of fluorescence intensity was almost negligible. This was mainly because some CDs may enter into the aqueous solution from the pores and then aggregate. When the concentration of N-doped CDs@ZIF-L was more than 2.5 mg mL−1, the fluorescent intensity was in a saturated state, which was mainly due to the poor solubility of the composite material. Therefore, a concentration of 2.5 mg mL–1 was selected for subsequent experiments.

The reaction time is an important parameter for fluorescence detection systems. To optimize the reaction time, the fluorescence intensity was measured after reacting for 0–5 min at room temperature. Owing to the significant concentration difference in the solution and the presence of adsorption sites on the surface of the composite material, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased within 3 min and then stabilized with increasing time (Fig. 7(b)). This phenomenon was mainly attributed to the large contact surface area of the 2D leaf structure of the N-doped CDs@ZIF-L, enabling TCs to exhibit more rapid kinetic properties in the composite material. However, this was owing to a reduction in the number of adsorption sites on the surface of the N-doped CDs@ZIF-L. In summary, a reaction time of 5 min was considered optimal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity for TCs through N-doped CDs@ZIF-L
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence spectra of N-doped CDs@ZIF-L were recorded under the optimal reaction conditions and in the presence of TCs. As shown in Fig. 7(c), the fluorescence intensity of N-doped CDs@ZIF-L at 420 nm gradually decreased with increasing TC concentration, and the emission peak gradually exhibited a red shift. When the concentration of TC was 70 μM, the blue fluorescence peak was quenched and the yellow peak become significant. Fluorescence quenching follows the Stern–Volmer equation [54]:
(3)
F 0/F = 1 + Kqτ0[C] = 1 + Ksv[C]
where F 0 and F denoted the fluorescence intensities of the N-doped CDs@ZIF-L in the absence and presence of the quencher, respectively; [C] was the concentration of the quencher; and Kq and Ksv was the quenching rate and Stern–Volmer quenching constants, respectively. From Fig. 7(d)–(f), it could be observed that a linear relationship was established between the fluorescence quenching efficiency (F/F0) and concentration of TC, DOC, and CTC ranging from 0–50 μM (R2 = 0.9941), 0–50 μM (R2 = 0.9982), and 0–45 μM (R2 = 0.9931), respectively. Furthermore, the corresponding limits of detection (LOD, LOD=3σ/k (n = 11), where σ was the standard deviation for the blank solution, and k was the slope of the calibration curve) were 0.025, 0.033, and 0.027 μM. The LODs of this detection system were lower than the maximum residue limits of TC in milk set by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA, maximum residue value = 0.676 μM) and the European Union (EU, maximum residue value = 0.225 μM) [55], demonstrating the feasibility of using N-doped CDs@ZIF-L for TCs detection. The quenching constants under the presence of TC, DOC, and CTC were determined to be 7.87 × 104, 1.26 × 105, and 9.96 × 104 L mol–1, respectively, and the quenching rate constants were 1.46 × 1013, 2.34 × 1013, and 1.85 × 1013 M–1 s−1, respectively. The value of Kq was significantly higher than 109–1010 M–1 s–1 [56], suggesting that static quenching may contribute to the quenching mechanism in this system. The prepared CDs showed the high QY without any additions and realized the utilization of fermentation residues. In comparison with other fluorescent methods for the TCs detection, the performances of sensing system were also comparable with great majority of reported fluorescence probes in Table S2. The results indicated that N-doped CDs were capable of using as a multifunctional sensing platform for the determination of TCs through fluorescence “turn off” modes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and detection mechanism of N-doped CDs@ZIF-L
ce:section.ce:para: In practical applications, high selectivity is an important parameter for evaluating the performances of N-doped CDs@ZIF-L fluorescent sensors. To assess the selectivity of the sensor, several common substances (Cr6+, Cr3+, Al3+, K+, Na+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Fe2+, AA, Gly, DL-Tar, L-His, L-Glu, D(-)-Fru, Cit, Suc, and Glu) were introduced under optimal experimental conditions. As shown in Fig. 8(a) and (b), some metal ions and substances such as Al3+, Fe3+, AA and Suc slightly affected the fluorescence intensity, but the fluorescence efficiency reduced significantly in the presence of TC, DOC, and CTC. The quenching effect of TCs was significant comparing with other interfering substances for N-doped CDs@ZIF-L fluorescent sensors. Therefore, this results indicated that the prepared N-doped CDs@ZIF-L exhibited good selectivity for TCs.

The interaction mechanism between the N-doped CDs@ZIF-L and different TCs was investigated. First, the N-doped CDs@ZIF-L exhibited adsorption and accumulation effects on TCs. After adding TC to N-doped CDs, the white suspension turned to yellow under sunlight (Fig. 8(c)), implying that ZIF-L could adsorb TCs because of its large outer surface area, thereby improving the adsorption capacity of the sensing system. Second, fluorescence quenching occurred between the N-doped CDs composite material and TCs. As shown in Fig. 8(d), the average fluorescence lifetime of the N-doped CDs was measured and determined to be 5.38, 5.23, 5.26, and 5.20 ns before and after adding TC, DOC, and CTC, respectively. The fluorescence lifetime of the N-doped CDs hardly changed in the presence of TCs, indicating that fluorescence changes occurred through static quenching. As shown in Fig. 8(c), an overlap was observed between the excitation wavelength of the N-doped CDs@ZIF-L and absorption peak of TCs, indicating the presence of an inner filter effect during the detection process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real tests in food samples for N-doped CDs@ZIF-L
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the feasibility and accuracy of the N-doped CDs@ZIF-L fluorescent sensor, the established method was used to detect three TCs in food samples. Different concentrations of TCs were added to the pre-treated honey and milk samples, and each concentration was measured in triplicate. The results were summarized in Table 2. The recoveries of the TCs, DOC, and CTC ranged between 87.12 % and 102.56 % and the relative standard deviations (RSD) ranged between 0.68 % and 3.16 %. These observations demonstrated the significant potential for further applications of the N-doped CDs@ZIF-L sensor in food safety and biological sample analyses.

In this study, biochar and N-doped CDs were prepared simultaneously by a one-pot hydrothermal carbonization method employing waste fermentation residues as the raw material. The obtained biochar exhibited an excellent adsorption capacity, with a maximum adsorption value of 693.22 mg g–1. Owing to the high nitrogen content of the raw material, the QY of N-doped CDs prepared under the optimal reaction conditions reached to 36.17 %. Compared to traditional 3D ZIF-8, the 2D leaf-shaped ZIF-L had a larger contact surface area and more abundant active sites, which was beneficial for improving the sensitivity of the detection process. Therefore, N-doped CDs were encapsulated in ZIF-L to form a new type of fluorescent probe. Notably, the N-doped CDs@ZIF-L exhibited good selectivity and stability performance for the TCs (TC, DOC, and CTC), in addition to a good linear relationships for TC, DOC, and CTC at concentrations ranging from 0–50, 0–50, and 0–45 μM, respectively. As a fluorescent probe, N-doped CDs@ZIF-L were used to detect TCs in honey and milk food samples using an established method, with recoveries of 87.12–102.56 %, indicating the practicality of this method and its significant potential for environmental monitoring and food safety analyses.
